Create beautiful
cross stitch patterns
from scratch or from a photo

Patternly is a free, browser-based pattern studio built by stitchers, for stitchers. Design stitch by stitch with layers and selections, convert any photo, match colours across five thread brands, and track your stitching on real kit charts — all in one place.

What you can do

Everything you need to design, convert & track

Create Patterns

Draw stitch by stitch on a customisable grid. Full, half, quarter, petite, backstitch, French knots — every technique you know, right here. Layers, a full selection tool, shape stamps, text effects, symmetry modes, and unlimited undo.

  • 12+ stitch types
  • Layers — draw over a photo safely
  • Select, move, rotate & copy regions
  • FLOW Score quality check

Convert Images

Turn any photo into a stitchable pattern in seconds. Upload an image, choose your thread brand and colour count, and Patternly generates a clean, stitch-ready chart matched to Luca-S, DMC, Anchor, Cosmo or Madeira threads.

  • Any photo or image
  • 5 thread brands matched
  • Live confetti analysis & cleanup
  • Styles, hue shift & fine-tuning

Stitch Tracker

Upload the chart and legend from any kit PDF and stitch straight off your screen. Tap to mark stitches done, highlight one colour at a time, and watch your progress climb — with thread amounts calculated for you.

  • Reads real kit PDF charts
  • Tap stitches to mark them done
  • Colour highlight "night vision"
  • Progress, strands & metres per colour
Simple by design

From idea to pattern in three steps

1

Open the editor

No sign-up. No install. Click "Open Editor" and you're in. Set your canvas size, choose your thread brand, and you're ready to create.

→
2

Design or convert

Draw freely with the full tool set, or upload a photo and let Patternly convert it into a clean, stitchable chart with matched thread colours.

→
3

Save & stitch

Save your pattern, export a print-ready PDF chart with symbols and a thread key, and get stitching. Your pattern, your threads, your way.

A trusted name in cross stitch

Built by people who stitch

Patternly is made by Luca-S — a cross stitch brand with decades of experience designing kits, threads, and tools that stitchers love. We built Patternly because we wanted a pattern editor that actually understands how cross stitching works, not just how pixels look on a screen.

When Luca-S puts their name on something, it means it's been made with care, tested by stitchers, and designed to last.

✓ Made by stitchers
✓ Free to use
✓ No account needed
✓ Works on any device

No sign-up. No install. Just open the editor and start creating. It's completely free.

Cross Stitch Calculators

Free planning tools — fabric size, stitching time, and thread quantities. No signup needed.

Pattern size

Fabric & margin

Extra fabric on each side for framing and finishing. 3" (≈8 cm) is the safe standard.
—
fabric to cut (with margin)
—stitched area (design only)
—total stitches (width × height)
—tapestry needle
—floss strands
—est. stitch time (100–200 st/hr)
Stitching over 2 threads on 28-count evenweave equals 14-count Aida. Time is a rough range — your pace may vary.

What does fabric count mean?

The "count" is how many stitches fit in one inch of fabric. A 100-stitch-wide design on 14-count fabric comes out about 7.1" wide; the same design on 18-count shrinks to about 5.6" — smaller stitches, same chart. Higher counts give finer detail but demand more precision and better lighting.

Fabric countBest forNeedleStrands
Aida 11Beginners, quick projects22–243
Aida 14Most patterns — the all-rounder24–262
Aida 16Slightly finer detail24–262
Aida 18Detailed work, smaller finish261–2
28 ct evenweave (over 2)Fine work, exposed-fabric designs26–282

Finished size at a glance

Pattern14-count16-count18-count
50 × 503.6" × 3.6"3.1" × 3.1"2.8" × 2.8"
100 × 1007.1" × 7.1"6.3" × 6.3"5.6" × 5.6"
150 × 15010.7" × 10.7"9.4" × 9.4"8.3" × 8.3"
200 × 20014.3" × 14.3"12.5" × 12.5"11.1" × 11.1"

Add 6–8 inches to each dimension for framing margins.

FAQ

How much extra fabric do I need for framing?

At least 3 inches per side — 6 inches added to each dimension in total. Go to 4 inches per side if you plan a scroll frame or a wide mat.

Can I use this for evenweave or linen?

Yes. Evenweave and linen are usually stitched "over two" threads, so divide the fabric count by two: 28-count over two behaves like 14-count Aida. Our dropdown handles this automatically.

Do fractional stitches change the size?

No. Quarter, half, and three-quarter stitches add detail inside the grid — a 100×100 pattern stays 100×100 cells regardless.

What is the difference between Aida and evenweave?

Aida has a visible grid of holes that makes counting easy. Evenweave is a finer, more uniform weave that looks like regular fabric — often chosen when unstitched fabric will show in the finished piece.

Know your size? Design the pattern.

Draw stitch by stitch or convert a photo — Patternly shows the finished size live as you set your canvas.

Project details

75%
2 h
—
estimated to complete
—total hours
—days at your pace
—months
—estimated stitches

How speed changes your timeline

Ranges account for ±25% speed uncertainty. Confetti-heavy patterns run at the high end — check your FLOW Score in the editor.

What is a realistic stitching speed?

Most stitchers settle between 100 and 160 stitches per hour at a comfortable pace. Beginners typically start around 60–80 while building muscle memory for counting and tension, and experienced stitchers using a stand and railroading techniques can sustain 250+. Burst speeds of 350–400 happen on big single-colour blocks, but they never survive contact with a real chart full of colour changes.

Want your personal number? Time one focused 30-minute session, count the completed stitches, and double it.

Why do projects always take longer than estimated?

Naive estimates divide stitch count by speed and stop there — that only measures needle-in-fabric time. Real projects include thread changes (30–60 seconds each, hundreds of times), chart-checking, breaks, and the occasional frogging session. That overhead typically adds 30–50% to a project, which is why this calculator applies a multiplier (×1.4 by default) instead of pretending it away.

Scattered single stitches — confetti — are the biggest time sink of all: each one costs a full thread change for one or two crosses. Two patterns with identical stitch counts can differ by 40+ hours purely on confetti. Patternly's Check tab measures exactly this with the FLOW Score and ConfettiScope, and the Confetti Cleanup tool fixes it before you commit.

Typical project times

ProjectStitchesHoursAt 1 h/day
Small motif (50×50) — ornaments, bookmarks1,500–2,50014–232–3 weeks
Medium piece (100×100) — samplers, small frames7,500–10,00070–932–3 months
Large project (200×200) — portraits, landscapes30,000–40,000280–3739–12 months
Full coverage (300×300) — big wall pieces67,500–90,000630–8401.5–2.5 years

Assumes 150 st/hr with ×1.4 overhead and ~75% coverage.

How to stitch faster

Use a stand. Freeing both hands is the single biggest speed upgrade most stitchers ever make. Fix your lighting — a daylight LED lamp makes counting faster and colours easier to tell apart. Work cross-country through colour blocks (finish one colour in an area before switching), and reduce confetti before you start: cutting a photo conversion from 40 colours to 20–25 often removes most scattered singles while keeping the picture readable.

FAQ

What overhead multiplier should I pick?

×1.4 is a sensible default for a normal pattern. Use ×1.2 for designs with large single-colour blocks and few colours; ×1.5 or more for confetti-heavy conversions with 30+ colours.

Does fabric count change how long it takes?

The stitch count is the same, so the theoretical time is too — but most stitchers report being 20–30% slower on 18-count than 14-count because the smaller holes demand more precision.

Is consistency or speed more important?

Consistency, by far. Thirty minutes every day finishes more projects than an eight-hour binge once a month — and your tension stays more even, too.

Does backstitch count toward the estimate?

No — backstitch, French knots, and specialty stitches aren't in the cross-stitch count, so a pattern with heavy outlining will run over the estimate. Budget extra time for it.

Check the FLOW Score before you commit.

Upload a photo in Patternly and see fragmentation, confetti, and workability scores in seconds — before you buy a single skein.

How much floss for one colour?

2
Standard: 2 strands for crosses on 14 ct, 1 strand for backstitch.
—
to buy (with 20% safety margin, rounded up to ¼ skein)
—thread needed
—in yards
—with 20% margin
—skeins (exact)

Thread per stitch by fabric count

How much floss is in a skein?

A standard Luca-S, DMC, or Anchor skein holds 8 metres of 6-strand floss; Madeira skeins hold 10 metres. Because you separate the strands before stitching, the usable length depends on how many strands you pull:

Strands usedBundles per skeinUsable length (8 m skein)
1 strand648 m
2 strands324 m
3 strands216 m
6 strands18 m

Why the 20% safety margin?

Real thread goes places calculators ignore: 2–3 cm of every cut length disappears into anchoring at the start and end, frogged thread is rarely reusable, and confetti stitches burn short runs with constant start/stop cycles. Most importantly, dye lots vary — a replacement skein bought months later can be a subtly different shade that shows in the finished piece. Buying all skeins of a colour at once, with margin, is the safe play.

How long should I cut my thread?

The classic answer is 18 inches (45 cm) — fingertips to elbow. Shorter lengths tangle less and stay fresher; longer lengths thin and fuzz as they pass through the fabric dozens of times. Go shorter for metallics and dark colours on light fabric. If you use the loop-start method, cut double (36") and fold — it only works with even strand counts.

FAQ

How many stitches does one skein cover?

On 14-count with 2 strands, roughly 960 full crosses per 8 m skein. On 18-count that rises to about 1,240 because each stitch is smaller. The table above adjusts for your exact settings.

Do I calculate backstitch separately?

Yes — backstitch usually uses 1 strand and consumes about 2 cm of thread per 1 cm of drawn line. Heavy outlining can add 10–20% to a colour's total.

Should I buy extra of dominant colours?

Yes. Backgrounds, skies, and skin tones are where a mid-project dye-lot switch is most visible. One extra skein of each dominant colour is cheap insurance.

Where do I find the stitch count per colour?

Patternly's Palette panel shows a live stitch count next to every colour in your pattern — exactly the number this calculator needs.

Get per-colour counts automatically.

Convert a photo or draw a pattern in Patternly — the palette shows every colour's stitch count, ready for this calculator.

About Thread Colour Conversion

Cross stitch kits and patterns usually name one brand's colours, but you might prefer or only have access to another. This hub finds the perceptually closest match between Luca-S, DMC and Anchor using Delta E colour distance (CIE76) in the CIELAB colour space.

Match quality is rated Excellent (ΔE under 3 — barely perceptible), Good (3–6 — noticeable but similar), Fair (6–10 — obviously different but related) or Approximate (over 10 — use with caution).

File Converter

Convert PCStitch .pat, open .oxs and Stitchmate .stitchmate charts into Patternly's .Ptly format — then download them, open them in the Editor, or start stitching them in the Tracker. Got a .xsd, .chart or other format? Drop it in anyway — we'll tell you exactly how to get it converted. Everything runs in your browser; your files never leave your device.

🔄
Tap to choose files or drag & drop — .xsm / .xsp / .pat / .oxs (several at once is fine)

Use your converted pattern right away

✏️
Open in the Editor

Every converted file gets an “✏️ Editor” button — one tap opens the pattern in the editor to recolour, add layers, or export a print-ready PDF.

🧵
Open in the Stitch Tracker

Or tap “🧵 Tracker” to load the chart with its full colour key into the Stitch Tracker and start marking stitches immediately.

Coming from another program?

Every stitching app has its own private format — but almost all of them can export OXS, the open interchange format Patternly reads natively. Export OXS from your program, then convert it here:

Pattern Maker (.xsd) — File → Export → OXS (or ask your designer for OXS/PDF)
WinStitch / MacStitch (.chart) — File → Export → OXS
PCStitch 7/8 (.pat) — File → Save As → PCStitch 5, or export OXS
Stitchmate (.stitchmate) — just drop it here, we convert it directly ✓
StitchFiddle, FlossCross & friends — download as OXS

Have a format we don't read and no way to export OXS? Send us a sample file — we add formats whenever they can be supported reliably.

What's .Ptly? Patternly's own pattern format — one small file your whole toolset understands: the editor, the tracker, and “Make a new pattern”.

Adjustments preview live and are baked into the photo when you convert. 💡 You can also fine-tune all of this after conversion — open Adjust Colors in the editor, where a paintable mask lets you adjust just one area.

Automatic clean-up
Strength — 50%
Confetti: —

Confetti — isolated single stitches — slows stitching with constant thread changes. Automatic clean-up replaces them with their strongest neighbouring colour. 💡 This can also be done after conversion: the editor's Confetti tool adds brush & mask control on top of the automatic clean-up.
